personality_voice.book_baseline: Book Harry is defined by a cluster of consistent traits: dry, deflecting humor under pressure (taunting Dudley with fake spells, sardonic responses to Lockhart); stubborn determination that overrides fear (insisting on staying at Hogwarts despite Dobby, refusing to leave the Quidditch match despite the rogue Bludger, going alone into the Chamber); a deep, sometimes painful self-consciousness about his fame (ducking behind books to avoid Lockhart, embarrassment at Colin's photos, discomfort at the Flourish and Blotts spectacle); genuine moral indignation that surfaces quickly (hot surge of anger at Malfoy insulting Weasleys, fury at Riddle framing Hagrid); and a recurring internal anxiety about his own identity — the Slytherin question, the Parseltongue revelation, the fear that he might be doing terrible things without knowing it. His voice is direct and short under stress, occasionally sarcastic, and he frequently thinks before speaking. He is also notably empathetic toward the powerless: his instinct to help Dobby, his guilt over Mr. Weasley's inquiry, his protectiveness toward Ginny.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core personality well. Harry's dry humor is present ('Now you've done it'; 'Be thankful it's not your neck'; 'Thanks, Myrtle' after her toilet offer; 'Looks like those Dueling Lessons came in handy'). His stubborn determination is intact across the Quidditch match, the forest, and the Chamber. His discomfort with fame is shown through body language and brief lines at Flourish and Blotts. His moral indignation appears when he confronts Lockhart ('You wrote them!') and defends Dumbledore to Riddle. The Parseltongue identity crisis is preserved, including the scene where he sits alone asking Hedwig 'Who am I?' — a moment not in the book but consistent with the book's internal anxiety. His empathy toward Dobby and Ginny is intact.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses some texture in Harry's internal voice. The book's extended internal monologue about the Slytherin question — lying awake, trying to speak Parseltongue alone, the Sorting Hat scene in Dumbledore's office carrying more weight — is compressed. The film adds the 'Who am I, Hedwig?' scene which is tonally consistent but not from the book. The book Harry's self-deprecating humor is slightly more frequent and sharper (e.g., the repeated 'I'll be in my bedroom making no noise' delivered with increasing deadpan). The film Harry is marginally more earnest and slightly less sardonic in small moments, but the overall pattern is preserved.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main traits — determination, dry humor, discomfort with fame, moral indignation, identity anxiety — are all present and recognizable. The loss is in frequency and texture of the sardonic voice and the depth of internal monologue. This warrants a minor deduction but not a substantial one, as the film portrayal is recognizably the same character.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Harry is the primary agent throughout. He initiates the investigation into the Chamber (proposes going to Hagrid, proposes using the Invisibility Cloak, leads the forest expedition, discovers the diary's interactive nature, deduces the basilisk from Hermione's note, decides to go to Lockhart, opens the Chamber, fights the basilisk alone, destroys the diary). He also drives several key social moments: he is the one who notices Dobby intercepted his letters, who figures out Dobby sealed the barrier, who confronts Lockhart, who frees Dobby. His agency is consistently active — he initiates, decides, and solves.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Harry's agency is largely preserved. He leads the forest expedition, opens the Chamber, fights the basilisk, destroys the diary, and frees Dobby. The key investigative breakthrough — reading Hermione's note about the basilisk — is preserved as Harry's deduction in the film. He confronts Lockhart, disarms him, and forces him into the Chamber. He goes alone past the rockfall to find Ginny.
narrative_role_agency.difference: Several specific acts of agency are transferred or reduced. In the books, Harry is the one who notices the paper in Hermione's hand and extracts it; in the film this is also present but the deduction scene is slightly compressed. More significantly, the book has Harry doing more active investigation throughout the year: he searches the corridor for clues after Mrs. Norris's attack, he goes to the library to research Riddle's award, he actively pursues Justin to explain the snake incident. In the film, some of these investigative beats are compressed or shown more passively. The Polyjuice interrogation is a joint effort in both, but Harry takes a slightly more active role in the book (he's the one who pushes the questioning of Malfoy, says 'You must have some idea'). The film also gives Hermione the 'Finite Incantatem' spell to stop the rogue Bludger — in the book Harry simply catches the Snitch and falls; Hermione's spell is a film addition that slightly reduces the chaos Harry navigates alone.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Harry remains the primary agent in all major plot-driving moments. The reductions are in investigative micro-agency during the middle of the story. No major action is fully transferred to another character. A modest deduction is warranted for the compression of his investigative initiative.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Harry's motivations are layered. The surface motivation is survival and protecting his friends (especially Hermione and Ginny). But beneath this is a deeper need: belonging. Hogwarts is explicitly 'the only place I've got — friends' and 'I belong in your world.' His desperation to return to Hogwarts is not just about school — it is about identity and home. The Slytherin conflict is a genuine internal crisis: he fears he might be evil, that the Sorting Hat was right, that his Parseltongue ability marks him as dark. He also carries guilt — about Mr. Weasley's inquiry, about Hermione being hurt while helping him. His motivation to go into the Chamber is explicitly tied to Ginny but also to the fact that he 'couldn't not go' — it is compulsive loyalty.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the belonging motivation clearly ('I don't belong here. I belong in your world — at Hogwarts. It's the only place I've got... friends'). The Slytherin internal conflict is present, including the 'Who am I?' scene and the Dumbledore conversation. The guilt over Hermione is present but less developed. The compulsive loyalty driving him into the Chamber is shown through his line 'We'll find her, Ron. Ginny's going to be fine' followed by the stage direction noting 'Even he doesn't believe what he's just said' — this is a film-specific addition that actually deepens the motivation slightly.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film loses some of the texture of Harry's guilt (the book has extended passages about his guilt over Mr. Weasley's inquiry, his guilt about Hermione). The internal conflict about Parseltongue and the Slytherin question is present but slightly less sustained — the book has Harry lying awake for hours, the film compresses this into a single outdoor scene. The motivation around belonging is well-preserved.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Core motivations are intact. The simplification is in the sustained texture of guilt and the depth of the Slytherin internal conflict across multiple scenes. This warrants a moderate deduction but not a severe one.

character_arc.book_baseline: Harry's arc in Chamber of Secrets is primarily about identity under doubt. He begins the year already famous and already belonging at Hogwarts, but the year systematically attacks his sense of self: he is suspected of being the Heir of Slytherin, he discovers he is a Parselmouth, the Sorting Hat confirms it would have put him in Slytherin, and Riddle explicitly draws parallels between them. The arc resolves with Dumbledore's 'It is our choices' speech — Harry's choice to ask not to be in Slytherin, and his ability to pull the sword from the hat, confirm his identity as a Gryffindor. The arc is about proving that identity is chosen, not determined by ability or circumstance. Secondary arc: Harry moves from isolation (no letters, locked up, no friends) to restored community.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The arc is present and reaches the same resolution. The Dumbledore conversation is preserved almost verbatim. The identity crisis is established through the Parseltongue incident, the Hufflepuff corridor scene, and the 'Who am I?' moment. The sword-from-the-hat moment and Dumbledore's speech close the arc correctly.
character_arc.difference: The arc is compressed. The book builds the identity crisis more gradually and more painfully — Harry's isolation at the Dursleys, the weeks of being suspected at school, the sustained anxiety about the Sorting Hat, the night lying awake. The film compresses the middle section significantly. Several steps of the arc — the sustained social ostracism, the extended guilt, the repeated moments of self-doubt — are present but abbreviated. The arc's shape is correct but its emotional accumulation is reduced.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc exists, reaches the correct resolution, and the key turning points are present. The compression reduces the emotional weight of the resolution somewhat. This is a case of a compressed but still faithful arc, warranting a score in the 11-13 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Harry's key relationships in this book: (1) Ron — equal partnership, mutual support, Ron's loyalty is tested and confirmed; (2) Hermione — intellectual partnership, Harry relies on her research, genuine friendship; (3) Hagrid — warmth, loyalty, Harry's protectiveness of Hagrid; (4) Dumbledore — trust, awe, Harry's need for Dumbledore's approval and belief; (5) Dobby — Harry's instinctive empathy toward the powerless, which drives the freeing of Dobby; (6) Ginny — Harry is largely oblivious to her feelings, treats her as Ron's little sister, but his protectiveness of her drives the climax.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Ron relationship is preserved — mutual support, shared humor, Ron's loyalty in the forest and Chamber. The Hermione relationship is preserved in function. The Hagrid relationship is present but slightly reduced (the book has more scenes at Hagrid's cabin, more warmth). The Dumbledore relationship is well-preserved in the office scene. The Dobby relationship is intact. The Ginny relationship is correctly portrayed as protective but not romantic.
key_relationships.difference: The Hagrid relationship loses some warmth — the book has multiple visits to Hagrid's cabin, the treacle fudge scene, Hagrid's teasing about signed photos. The film reduces Hagrid to fewer scenes. The Ron relationship loses some of the book's texture of shared humor and mutual teasing across the year. These are compressions rather than distortions of the emotional dynamic.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key dynamics are preserved in their essential character. The compressions reduce texture but do not change the nature of any relationship. A minor deduction for the reduced Hagrid warmth and the compressed Ron dynamic.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: The book Harry has significant complexity that goes beyond the main plot: his class-consciousness about the Weasleys' poverty (feeling awkward about his own vault); his petty satisfaction at Malfoy's humiliations; his willingness to use Dudley's fear of magic for entertainment before guilt sets in; his lie to Dumbledore about why he was in the corridor (protecting Hagrid's secret about the car); his self-aware recognition that he is not the top student ('that's Hermione'); his complicated relationship with fame — simultaneously embarrassed by it and aware of its uses; his instinct to protect people weaker than himself even at cost to himself. The book also shows Harry making mistakes: he doesn't think to send Hedwig with a message when locked out of the platform, he rises to Dudley's bait and gets punished, he misjudges the Dobby situation repeatedly.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the embarrassment about fame, the protectiveness toward the powerless, the self-deprecating humor, and the willingness to break rules for loyalty. The lie to Dumbledore is present in the film. The class-consciousness about the Weasleys' poverty is absent from the film. The petty satisfaction at Malfoy's humiliations is present in small moments. The 'that's Hermione' self-deprecation is absent from the film.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: Lost material includes: the extended Dursley summer establishing Harry's isolation and longing; the class-consciousness about his Gringotts vault versus the Weasleys'; the taunting of Dudley with fake spells and subsequent guilt; the sustained social ostracism scenes in the library and corridors; the Kwikspell discovery and Harry's empathy for Filch; the Nearly Headless Nick deathday party as a scene showing Harry's social loyalty (he keeps a promise at personal cost); the extended Riddle diary investigation (trophy room visit, sustained curiosity about Riddle); the Valentine's Day humiliation scene which shows Harry's vulnerability to public embarrassment. Several of these absences reduce the texture of Harry's characterization without being replaced by equivalent material.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The lost material is substantial in volume but the most characterization-defining elements (identity crisis, loyalty, determination, empathy) are preserved. What is lost is primarily texture and secondary complexity — the class-consciousness, the extended social ostracism, the Filch empathy, the deathday party loyalty. These losses flatten Harry somewhat but do not fundamentally misrepresent him. A score in the 10-11 range is appropriate.

lost_or_transferred_material: Harry's extended internal monologue about Slytherin identity across multiple nights
Harry's class-consciousness about his Gringotts vault versus the Weasleys' poverty
Harry taunting Dudley with fake spells and subsequent guilt/punishment
Harry's self-deprecating acknowledgment that Hermione is top of the year, not him
Harry keeping his promise to attend Nearly Headless Nick's deathday party at personal cost
Harry's active investigation of Riddle's trophy and award in the trophy room
Harry's empathy for Filch upon discovering the Kwikspell letter
Harry's sustained guilt over Mr. Weasley's Ministry inquiry
The Valentine's Day dwarf scene showing Harry's vulnerability to public humiliation
Harry lying awake for hours processing the Parseltongue revelation
Harry's petty satisfaction at Malfoy's humiliations in Borgin and Burkes
Hermione's 'Finite Incantatem' stopping the rogue Bludger (book: Harry simply catches Snitch and falls; film transfers this spell to Hermione)
Harry's extended social ostracism scenes in library and corridors across multiple weeks
key_observations: The film adaptation of Harry Potter in Chamber of Secrets is substantially faithful. The core identity — determined, loyal, dry-humored, uncomfortable with fame, driven by belonging — is preserved across both corpora. The major plot-driving acts of agency (opening the Chamber, fighting the basilisk, destroying the diary, freeing Dobby) are all retained. The central arc about chosen identity versus innate ability reaches the same resolution with the same dialogue. The primary losses are in texture and accumulation: the sustained internal anxiety about Slytherin identity, the class-consciousness, the secondary relationships (particularly Hagrid's warmth), and the numerous small scenes that build Harry's complexity across the year. The film Harry is recognizably the same character but somewhat simplified — more consistently heroic and less frequently petty, guilty, or self-doubting in the small moments that give the book version its richness. No major action or emotional beat is transferred to another character, and no false traits are added. The score of 79 reflects a faithful but compressed adaptation that preserves the essential character while losing meaningful secondary texture.
